author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

title: Homily XXIII on Acts x. 23, 24.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then called he them in, and lodged them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And on the morrow Peter went away with them, and certain brethren from Joppa accompanied him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the morrow after they entered into Cæsarea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Cornelius waited for them, and had called together his kinsmen and near friends.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He” called them in, and lodged them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Good, that first he gives the men friendly treatment, after the fatigue of their journey, and makes them at home with him; “and on the morrow,” sets out with them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And certain accompany him: this too as Providence ordered it, that they should be witnesses afterwards when Peter would need to justify himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Cornelius was waiting for them, and had called together his kinsmen and near friends.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the part of a friend, this the part of a devout man, that where such blessings are concerned, he takes care that his near friends shall be made partakers of all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of course (his “near” friends), those in whom he had ever full confidence; fearing, with such an interest at stake, to entrust the matter to others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In my opinion, it was by Cornelius himself that both friends and kinsmen had been brought to a better mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as Peter was coming in, Cornelius met him, and fell down at his feet, and worshipped him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, both to teach the others, and by way of giving thanks to God, and showing his own humility: thereby making it plain, that though he had been commanded, yet in himself he had great piety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then did Peter?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Peter took him up, saying, Stand up; I myself also am a man.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how, before all else (the Apostles) teach them this lesson, not to think great things of them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as he talked with him, he went in, and found many that were come together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said unto them, Ye know how that it is an unlawful thing for a man that is a Jew to keep company, or come unto one of another nation; but God hath showed me that I should not call any man common or unclean.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, he straightway speaks of the mercy of God, and points out to them that it is a great grace that God has shown them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe also how while he utters great things, at the same time he speaks modestly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he does not say, We, being men who do not deign to keep company with any (such), have come to you: but what says he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye know”—God commanded this—“that it is against law to keep company with, or come unto, one of another nation.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he goes on to say, “And to me God has shown”—this he says, that none may account the thanks due to him—“that I should call no man”—that it may not look like obsequiousness to him, “no human being,” says he—“common or unclean.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wherefore also”—that they may not think the affair a breach of the law on his part, nor (Cornelius) suppose that because he was in a station of command therfore he had complied, but that they may ascribe all to God,—“wherefore also I came without gainsaying as soon as I was sent for:” (though) not only to keep company, but even to come unto (him) was not permitted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I ask therefore, for what intent ye have sent for me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Already Peter had heard the whole matter from the soldiers also, but he wishes them first to confess, and to make them amenable to the Faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then does Cornelius?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, Why, did not the soldiers tell thee? but observe again, how humbly he speaks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he says, “From the fourth day I was fasting until this hour; and at the ninth hour I prayed in my house, and, behold, a man stood before me in bright clothing, and said, Cornelius, thy prayer is heard, and thine alms are come up for a memorial before God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And at the ninth hour,” he says, “I was praying.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me, that this man had also fixed for himself set times of a life under stricter rule, and on certain days.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is why he he says, “From the fourth day.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how great a thing prayer is!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he advanced in piety, then the Angel appears to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “From the fourth day:” i.e. of the week; not “four days ago.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “on the morrow Peter went away with them, and on the morrow after they entered into Cæsarea:” this is one day: and the day on which the persons sent came (to Joppa) one day: and on the third (the Angel) appeared: so that there are two days after that on which (Cornelius) had been praying.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And, behold, a man stood before me in bright clothing:” he does not say, an Angel, so unassuming is he: “and said, Cornelius, thy prayer is heard, and thine alms are had in remembrance in the sight of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Send therefore to Joppa, and call hither Simon, whose surname is Peter: he is lodged in the house of one Simon a tanner by the seaside: who, when he cometh, shall speak unto thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Immediately therefore I sent to thee; and thou hast well done that thou art come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now therefore are we all here present before God, to hear all things that are commanded thee of God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 31–33.) (b) See what faith, what piety!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He knew that it was no word of man that Peter spake, when he said, “God hath shown me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then says the man, “We are present to hear all things that are commanded thee of the Lord. (a) Therefore it was that Peter asked, “For what intent have ye sent for me?” on purpose that he might so speak these very words. (d) “Then Peter opened his mouth, and said, Of a truth I perceive that God is no respector of persons: but in every nation he that feareth Him, and worketh righteousness, is acceptable to him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That is, be he uncircumcised or circumcised. (c) This also Paul declaring, saith, “For there is no respect of persons with God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11.) (e) What then? (it may be asked) is the man yonder in Persia acceptable to Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he be worthy, in this regard he is acceptable, that it should be granted him to be brought unto faith (τᾥ καταξιωθἥναι τῆς πίστεως).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Eunuch from Ethiopia He overlooked not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What shall one say then of the religious men who have been overlooked?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not the case, that any (such) ever was overlooked.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what he says is to this effect, that God rejects no man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In every nation, he that feareth God and worketh righteousness:” (by righteousness) he means, all virtue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark, how he subdues all elation of mind in him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That (the Jews) may not seem to be in the condition of persons cast off (he adds), “The word which He sent unto the children of Israel, preaching peace by Jesus Christ: He is Lord of all (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36): this he says also for the sake of those present (of the Jews), that He may persuade them also: this is why he forces Cornelius to speak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He,” saith he, “is Lord of all.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe at the very outset, “The word,” says he, “which He sent unto the children of Israel;” he gives them the preëminence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he adduces (these Gentiles) themselves as witnesses: “ye know,” says he, “the matter which came to pass throughout all Judea, beginning at Galilee”—then he confirms it from this also—“after the baptism which John preached” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 37)—“even Jesus of Nazareth, how God anointed Him with the Holy Ghost and with power.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not mean, Ye know Jesus, for they did not know Him, but he speaks of the things done by Him: “Who went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil: by this he shows that many cases of lost senses or paralyzed limbs are the devil’s work, and a wrench given to the body by him: as also Christ said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For God was with Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, lowly terms.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And we are witnesses of all things which He did, both in the country of the Jews, and in Jerusalem” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 39): both “we,” saith he, and ye.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the Passion, and the reason why they do not believe: “Whom also they slew, and hanged on a tree.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Him God raised up the third day, and showed Him openly; not to all the people, but unto witnesses chosen before of God, even to us, who did eat and drink with Him after He rose from the dead.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is a proof of the Resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he commanded us to preach unto the people, and to testify that it is He which was ordained of God to be the Judge of quick and dead.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he adduces the testimony from the Prophets: “To Him give all the prophets witness, that through His name, whosoever believeth in Him shall receive remission of sins.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is a proof of that which was about to be: this is the reason why he here cites the Prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what relates to Cornelius.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He sent, it says, to Joppa to fetch Peter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He was waiting for him,” etc; see how fully he believed that Peter would certainly come: (b) “and fell down at his feet, and worshipped him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24, 25.) (a) Mark how on every side it is shown how worthy he is!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (So) the Eunuch there desired Philip to come up and sit in the chariot (ch. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 31), although not knowing who he was, upon no other introduction (ἐπαγγελίας) than that given by the Prophet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here Cornelius fell at his feet. (c) “Stand up, I myself also am a man.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how free from adulation his speech is on all occasions, and how full of humility.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And conversing with him, he came in.” (a) (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Conversing about what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I suppose saying these words: “I myself also am a man.” (e) Do you mark (Peter’s) unassuming temper?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He himself also shows that his coming is God’s doing: “Ye know that it is unlawful for a man that is a Jew,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why did he not speak of the linen sheet?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe Peter’s freedom from all vainglory: but, that he is sent of God, this indeed he mentions; of the manner in which he was sent, he speaks not at present; when the need has arisen, seeing he had said, “Ye know that it is unlawful for a man that is a Jew to keep company with, or to come unto, one of another nation,” he simply adds, “but to me God hath shown,” etc. There is nothing of vainglory here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “All ye,” he says, “know.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He makes their knowledge stand surety for him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Cornelius says, “We are present before God to hear all things that are commanded thee of the Lord” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 33): not, Before man, but, “Before God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the way one ought to attend to God’s servants.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see his awakened mind? do you see how worthy he was of all these things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Peter,” it says, “opened his mouth, and said, Of a truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This he said also by way of justifying himself with the Jews then present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, being at the point to commit the Word to these (Gentiles), he first puts this by way of apology.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was He “a respecter of persons” beforetime?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For beforetime likewise it was just the same: “Every one,” as he saith, “that feareth Him, and worketh righteousness, would be acceptable to Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As when Paul saith, “For when the Gentiles which have not the Law, do by nature the things of the Law.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That feareth God and worketh righteousness:” he assumes both doctrine and manner of life: is “accepted with Him;” for, if He did not overlook the Magi, nor the Ethiopian, nor the thief, nor the harlot, much more them that work righteousness, and are willing, shall He in anywise not overlook.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What say you then to this, that there are likely persons (ἐπιεικεἵς), men of mild disposition, and yet they will not believe?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, you have yourself named the cause: they will not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But besides the likely person he here speaks of is not this sort of man, but the man “that worketh righteousness:” that is, the man who in all points is virtuous and irreproachable, when he has the fear of God as he ought to have it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But whether a person be such, God only knows.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how this man was acceptable: see how, as soon as he heard, he was persuaded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yes, and now too,” say you, “every one would be persuaded, be who he may.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the signs that are now, are much greater than those, and more wonderful.—Then Peter commences his teaching, and reserves for the Jews the privilege of their birth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The word,” he says, “which He sent unto the children of Israel, preaching peace (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36), not bringing judgment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is sent to the Jews also: yet for all this He did not spare them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Preaching peace through Jesus Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is Lord of all.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: First he discourses of His being Lord and in exceeding elevated terms, seeing he had to deal with a soul more than commonly elevated, and that took all in with ardor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he proves how He was Lord of all, from the things which He achieved “throughout all Judea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For ye know,” saith he, “the matter which came to pass throughout all Judea:” and, what is the wonderful part of it, “beginning at Galilee: after the baptism which John preached.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: First he speaks of His success, and then again he says concerning Him, “Jesus of Nazareth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, what a stumbling-block, this birthplace!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “How God anointed Him with the Holy Ghost and with power.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again the proof—how does that appear?—from the good that He did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil:” and the greatness of the power shown when He overcomes the devil; and the cause, “Because God was with Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore also the Jews spake thus: “We know that Thou art a teacher come from God: for none can do these miracles except God be with him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, when he has shown that He was sent from God, he next speaks of this, that He was slain: that thou mayest not imagine aught absurd.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou how far they are from hiding the Cross out of view, nay, that together with the other circumstances they put also the manner?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whom also,” it says, “they slew by hanging on a tree.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And gave Him,” it is added, “to be made manifest not to all the people, but to witnesses before ordained of God, even unto us:” and yet it was (Christ) Himself that elected them; but this also he refers to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To the before-ordained,” he says, “even to us, who did eat and drink with Him after that He was risen from the dead.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See whence he fetches his assurance of the resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is the reason that being risen he did no sign, but only ate and drank?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because the Resurrection itself was a great sign, and of this nothing was so much a sign as the eating and drinking.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To testify,” saith he—in a manner calculated to alarm—that they may not have it in their power to fall back upon the excuse of ignorance: and he does not say, “that He is the Son of God,” but, what would most alarm them, “that it is He which is ordained of God, to be the Judge of quick and dead.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To him give all the Prophets witness,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When by the terror he has agitated them, then he brings in the pardon, not spoken from himself but from the Prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what is terrifying is from him, what is mild from the Prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All ye that have received this forgiveness, all ye to whom it has been vouchsafed to attain unto faith, learn, I beseech you, the greatness of the Gift, and study not to be insolent to your Benefactor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For we obtained forgiveness, not that we should become worse, but to make us far better and more excellent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let none say that God is the cause of our evil doings, in that He did not punish, nor take vengeance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If (as it is said) a ruler having taken a murderer, lets him go, say, is he (not) judged to be the cause of the murders afterwards committed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See then, how we expose God to the tongues of the wicked.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what do they not say, what leave unuttered? “(God) Himself,” say they, “allowed them; for he ought to have punished them as they deserved, not to honor them, nor crown them, nor admit them to the foremost privileges, but to punish and take vengeance upon them: but he that, instead of this, honors them, has made them to be such as they are.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not, I beseech and implore you, do not let any man utter such speech as far as we are concerned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Better to be buried ten thousand times over, than that God through us should be so spoken of!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews, we read, said to (Christ) Himself, “Thou that destroyest the Temple, and in three days buildest it up, come down from the Cross” (Matt. xxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 40): and again, “If Thou be the Son of God:” but the reproaches here are more grievous than those, that through us He should be called a teacher of wickedness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us cause the very opposite to be said, by having our conversation worthy of Him that calleth us, and (worthily) approaching to the baptism of adoption.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For great indeed is the might of baptism (φωτίσματου): it makes them quite other men than they were, that partake of the gift; it does not let the men be men (and nothing more).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make thou the Gentile (τὸν ῞Ελληνα), to believe that great is the might of the Spirit, that it has new-moulded, that it has fashioned thee anew.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why waitest thou for the last gasp, like a runaway slave, like a malefactor, as though it were not thy duty to live unto God?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why dost thou stand affected to Him, as if thou hadst in Him a ruthless, cruel Master?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What can be more heartless (ψυχρότερον), what more miserable, than those who make that the time to receive baptism?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God made thee a friend, and vouchsafed thee all His good things, that thou mayest act the part of a friend.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Suppose you had done some man the greatest of wrongs, had insulted him, and brought upon him disgraces without end, suppose you had fallen into the hands of the person wronged, and he, in return for all this, had honored you, made you partaker of all that he had, and in the assembly of his friends, of those in whose presence he was insulted, had crowned you, and declared that he would hold you as his own begotten son, and then straightway had died: say, would you not have bewailed him? would you not have deemed his death a calamity? would you not have said, Would that he were alive, that I might have it in my power to make the fit return, that I might requite him, that I might show myself not base to my benefactor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then, where it is but man, this is how you would act; and where it is God, are you eager to be gone, that you may not requite your benefactor for so great gifts?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay rather, choose the time for coming to Him so that you shall have it in your power to requite Him like for like.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, say you, but I cannot keep (the gift).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Has God commanded impossibilities?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence it is that all is clean reversed, hence that, all the world over, every thing is marred—because nobody makes it his mark to live after God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus those who are yet Catechumens, because they make this their object, (how they may defer baptism to the last,) give themselves no concern about leading an upright life: and those who have been baptized (φωτισθεντες), whether it be because they received it as children, or whether it be that having received it in sickness, and afterwards recovered (ἀνενεγκόντες), they had no hearty desire to live on (to the glory of God), so it is, that neither do these make an earnest business of it: nay, even such as received it in health, have little enough to show of any good impression, and warmly affected for the time, these also presently let the fire go out.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do you flee? why do you tremble? what is it you are afraid of?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You do not mean to say that you are not permitted to follow your business?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not part you from your wife!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, it is from fornication that I bar you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not debar you from the enjoyment of your wealth?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, but from covetousness and rapacity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not oblige you to empty out all your coffers?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, but to give some small matter according to your means to them that lack, your superfluities to their need, and not even this unrewarded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We do not urge you to fast?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We do but forbid you to besot yourselves with drunkenness and gormandizing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The things we would retrench are but the very things which bring you disgrace; things which even here, on this side of hell-fire, you yourselves confess to be things to be shunned and hated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We do not forbid you to be glad and to rejoice?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, only rejoice not with a disgraceful and unbecoming merriment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is it you dread, why are you afraid, why do you tremble?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where marriage is, where enjoyment of wealth, where food in moderation, what matter of sin is there in these things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, they that are without enjoin the opposites to these, and are obeyed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they demand not according to thy means, but they say, Thou must give thus much: and if thou allege poverty, they will make no account of that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so Christ: Give, saith He, of what thou hast, and I inscribe thee in the first rank.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again those say, If thou wilt distinguish thyself, forsake father, mother, kindred, friends, and keep close attendance on the Palace, laboring, toiling, slaving, distracted, suffering miseries without number.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so Christ; but keep thou, saith He, at home with thy wife, with thy children, and as for thy daily occupations reform and regulate them on the plan of leading a peaceable life, free from cares and from perils.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, say you, but the other promises wealth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Aye, but Christ a kingdom, and more, He promises wealth also with it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “Seek ye,” saith He, “the kingdom of Heaven, and all these things shall be added unto you” (Matt. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 33): throwing in, by way of additional boon, what the other holds out as the main thing: and the Psalmist says, he has “never seen the righteous forsaken, nor his seed begging their bread.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ps. xxxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us set about practising virtue, let us make a beginning; let us only lay hold on it, and you shall see what the good will be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For surely in these (worldly) objects you do not succeed so without labor, that you should be so faint-hearted for these (higher) objects—that you should say, Those are to be had without labor, these only with toil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay,—what need to tell you what is the true state of the case?—those are had only with greater labor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not recoil from the Divine Mysteries, I beseech you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Look not at this, that one who was baptized before thee, has turned out ill, and has fallen from his hope: since among soldiers also we see some not doing their duty by the service, while we see others distinguishing themselves, and we do not look only at the idle ones, but we emulate these, the men who are successful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But besides, consider how many, after their baptism, have of men become angels!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fear the uncertainty of the future.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As a thief in the night,” so death comes: and not merely as a thief, but while we sleep it sets upon us, and carries us off while we are idling.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To this end has God made the future uncertain, that we may spend our time in the practice of virtue, because of the uncertainty of expectation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But He is merciful, say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How long shall we hear this senseless, ridiculous talk?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I affirm not only that God is merciful, but that nothing can be more merciful than He, and that He orders all things concerning us for our good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many all their life do you see afflicted with the worst form of leprosy! (ἐν ἐλέφαντι διάγοντας, “Elephantiasis,”) how many blind from their earliest youth even to old age! others who have lost their eyesight, others in poverty, others in bonds, others again in the mines, others entombed (καταχωσθέντας) together, others (slaughtered) in wars!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things say you, do not look like mercy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, could He not have prevented these things had He wished, yet He permits them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, those who are blind from their infancy, why are they so?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I will not tell you, until you promise me to receive baptism, and, being baptized, to live aright.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not right to give you the solution of these questions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The preaching is not meant just for amusement.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even if I solve this, on the back of this follows another question: of such questions there is a bottomless deep.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore do not get into a habit of looking to have them solved for you: else we shall never stop questioning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For look, if I solve this, I do but lead the way to question upon question, numberless as the snowflakes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that this is what we learn, rather to raise questions, not to solve the questions that are raised.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even if we do solve them, we have not solved them altogether, but (only) as far as man’s reasoning goes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The proper solution of such questions is faith: the knowing that God does all things justly and mercifully and for the best: that to comprehend the reason of them is impossible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the one solution, and another better than this exists not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, what is the use of having a question solved?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, that one needs no longer to make a question of the thing which is solved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if thou get thyself to believe this, that all things are ordered by the Providence of God, Who, for reasons known to Himself, permits some things and actively works others, thou art rid of the need of questioning, and hast gotten the gain of the solution.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us come back to our subject.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see such numbers of men suffering chastisements?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God (say you) permits these things to be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make the right use of the health of the body, in order to the health of the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But you will say, What is the use to me of labors and toil, when it is in my power to get quit of all (my sins) without labor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the first place, this is not certain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It may happen, that a person not only does not get quit of his sins without labor, but that he departs hence with all his sins upon him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, even if this were certain, still your argument is not to be tolerated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has drawn thee to the contests: the golden arms lie there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When you ought to take them, and to handle them, you wish to be ingloriously saved, and to do no good work!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, if war broke out, and the Emperor were here, and you saw some charging into the midst of the phalanxes of the enemy, hewing them down, dealing wounds by thousands, others thrusting (with the sword’s point), others bounding (now here, now there), others dashing on horseback, and these praised by the Emperor, admired, applauded, crowned: others on the contrary thinking themselves well off if they take no harm, and keeping in the hindmost ranks, and sitting idly there; then after the close of the war, the former sort summoned, honored with the greatest gifts, their names proclaimed by the heralds: while of the latter, not even the name becomes known, and their reward of the good obtained is only that they are safe: which sort would you wish to belong to?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, if you were made of stone, if you were more stupid even than senseless and lifeless things, would you not ten thousand times rather belong to the former?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yea, I beseech and implore you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if need were to fall fighting, ought you not eagerly to choose this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See you not how it is with them that have fallen in the wars, how illustrious they are, how glorious?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet they die a death, after which there is no getting honor from the emperor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in that other war, there is nothing of the kind, but thou shalt in any wise be presented with thy scars.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which scars, even without persecutions, may it be granted all us to have to exhibit, through Jesus Christ our Lord, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, and world without end.
